input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: Microsoft Surface Book 137 14.2" 48GB RAM 1024GB SSD
input.description: The Microsoft Surface Book delivers AMD Ryzen 9 7940HS performance with NVIDIA RTX 4070 graphics. 48GB of RAM and a 1024GB SSD on a 14.2-inch 3840x2400 display. Up to 8 hours of battery life. Ships in Silver.

raw_attributes.description: The Microsoft Surface Book delivers AMD Ryzen 9 7940HS performance with NVIDIA RTX 4070 graphics. 48GB of RAM and a 1024GB SSD on a 14.2-inch 3840x2400 display. Up to 8 hours of battery life. Ships in Silver.

specialist_capabilities: High-performance CPU + GPU: 8-core AMD Ryzen 9 7940HS paired with an NVIDIA RTX 4070 for gaming, GPU-accelerated rendering, and compute-heavy tasks.
Large memory & storage: 48 GB RAM and a 1,024 GB SSD for heavy multitasking, large projects, and fast file access.
High-resolution display: 14.2" 3840×2400 panel (~318 ppi) — very high pixel density for detailed previewing and layout work.
Portability class: desktop-replacement weight (~6.1 lb / 2.8 kg) — not an ultraportable; better for workstation-on-the-go than frequent travel.
Battery: manufacturer-claimed up to 8 hours — moderate endurance; expect much lower runtime under sustained CPU/GPU load.
OS & enterprise readiness: ships with Windows 11 Pro, suitable for business software, domain/IT management and enterprise features.
Unknowns to verify pre-purchase: GPU VRAM, display panel type (IPS/OLED) and color gamut, port selection (Thunderbolt/USB/SD), and serviceability/upgradeability.

specialist_audience.students: Good for advanced students (design/CS/engineering) who need GPU horsepower; heavy for everyday campus carry.
specialist_audience.professionals: Fits professionals requiring Windows Pro, strong CPU/GPU performance and lots of RAM for productivity and enterprise use.
specialist_audience.developers: Well-suited for local VMs, builds and multitasking due to high core count, large RAM, and fast SSD.
specialist_audience.gamers: Attractive for gamers who want a powerful GPU in a smaller footprint, but check sustained thermals and battery impact.
specialist_audience.creators: Strong fit for photo/video/3D creators thanks to high-res screen and RTX acceleration; verify panel color accuracy for color-critical work.
specialist_audience.casual_home: Overpowered and heavy for casual browsing/streaming — likely more machine than needed.
specialist_audience.power_users: Excellent for power users handling large datasets, virtualization, and heavy multitasking.

specialist_buyer_questions: What is the amount of dedicated VRAM on the NVIDIA RTX 4070 in this configuration?
Is the 14.2" display IPS, OLED, or another panel type, and what color gamuts (sRGB/Adobe RGB/DCI-P3) and Delta-E specs does it meet?
Are the screen and keyboard dock detachable (is this a 2-in-1) or fixed? If detachable, what is the tablet mode battery/runtime?
What ports are included (Thunderbolt/USB-C, USB-A, HDMI, SD card slot, Ethernet via adapter)?
Is RAM or storage user-upgradeable/replaceable after purchase?
What are real-world battery runtimes for light use vs. heavy CPU/GPU workloads?
How does the chassis handle thermals—any reported throttling during long gaming or rendering sessions?
What are the exact physical dimensions and hinge/weight distribution for carrying comfort?
Does it include a stylus or support Microsoft Surface Pen input, and is pen latency specified?
Is there a built-in IR webcam/fingerprint reader for Windows Hello and business security?
What is the warranty period and are there options for on-site or extended business support?
Are enterprise features like BitLocker, TPM, and remote management pre-enabled or supported?
Is Wi-Fi 6E / Wi-Fi 6 and Bluetooth version specified?
Are there any vendor-supplied benchmarks or thermal profiles for sustained GPU loads?
Does Microsoft provide repairability/serviceability details (e.g., battery replacement, keyboard/screen repair)?

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

You classify e-commerce products by product type. Return JSON with keys:
  product_type     short noun (e.g. 'laptop', 'blender', 'headphones', 'smart-bulb', 'office-chair')
  taxonomy_path    list of broader categories from generic to specific
                   (e.g. ['electronics','computers','laptop'])
  confidence       0.0–1.0 — your certainty
If the input is too sparse to classify, return product_type='unknown' and confidence below 0.3. Never invent specs — only label.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: Acer Swift 020 17.3" 16GB RAM 256GB SSD
input.description: The Acer Swift delivers Intel Core i5-1340P performance with AMD Radeon 780M graphics. 16GB of RAM and a 256GB SSD on a 17.3-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 17 hours of battery life. Ships in Platinum.

raw_attributes.description: The Acer Swift delivers Intel Core i5-1340P performance with AMD Radeon 780M graphics. 16GB of RAM and a 256GB SSD on a 17.3-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 17 hours of battery life. Ships in Platinum.

specialist_capabilities: Productivity CPU: Intel Core i5-1340P (12 cores, up to 4.6 GHz) — solid for office apps, multitasking and coding workloads
Graphics: AMD Radeon 780M — capable for accelerated UI, video playback and light/entry-level gaming or GPU-accelerated photo edits, not a high-end discrete GPU
Memory & storage balance: 16 GB RAM and 256 GB SSD — good for everyday multitasking and fast boot, but limited for large media projects or big local datasets
Large high-res screen: 17.3" 2560×1600 display — more screen real estate and sharper text (~175 ppi) for spreadsheets, documents and multitasking
Portability class: mainstream portable (~3.34 lb / 1.52 kg) — easier to carry than typical 17" desktop replacements
Battery: up to 17 hours (manufacturer claim) — optimized for all-day productivity or travel with mixed/light use
OS: Windows 11 Pro — business features (BitLocker, Group Policy) and broad app compatibility

specialist_audience.students: Large screen and long battery life suit studying, multitasking and note-taking; 16 GB is sufficient for most coursework
specialist_audience.professionals: Windows 11 Pro and long battery life make it suitable for office work, remote meetings and business tasks
specialist_audience.developers: Strong multicore i5 and 16 GB RAM handle coding, local builds and virtualization at moderate scale
specialist_audience.gamers: Fits casual or light gamers who want a large display; GPU is not a substitute for a dedicated gaming GPU
specialist_audience.creators: Entry-level photo/video editing on a high-res screen is practical, but limited storage/RAM and unknown panel color accuracy constrain heavy creative work
specialist_audience.casual_home: Great for streaming, browsing and general media on a large, sharp screen
specialist_audience.power_users: May be limiting for heavy content creation, large datasets or ML workloads due to 16 GB RAM and 256 GB storage

specialist_buyer_questions: Is the Radeon 780M a discrete GPU or integrated/part of the CPU/platform in this model?
What type of display panel is used (IPS, OLED, or other) and what is the color gamut (sRGB/Adobe RGB coverage)?
What is the display's refresh rate (60 Hz, 120 Hz, etc.)?
Are RAM and SSD user-upgradeable (soldered RAM vs. SO-DIMM, M.2 slot availability)?
Is there a spare storage slot or only a single 256 GB drive?
Which ports are present (USB-C, Thunderbolt 4, HDMI, SD card reader) and does USB-C support charging or DisplayPort alt mode?
What are real-world battery figures for video playback, web browsing and office work (manufacturer 'up to' figures vs. measured)?
How is thermal performance under sustained CPU/GPU load (throttling, fan noise)?
What are the webcam resolution and microphone quality for video calls?
Is there a backlit keyboard and what is the layout (US/international) and key travel like?
Does the laptop include a fingerprint reader or IR camera for Windows Hello?
What is the exact weight and chassis dimensions for travel planning?
What warranty and support options are included (duration, on-site vs. depot)?
Does Windows 11 Pro come activated and are there any preinstalled OEM apps affecting storage?
Is there any factory bloatware that should be removed for maximum storage/performance?

  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

  product_type     short noun (e.g. 'laptop', 'blender', 'headphones', 'smart-bulb', 'office-chair')
  taxonomy_path    list of broader categories from generic to specific
                   (e.g. ['electronics','computers','laptop'])
  confidence       0.0–1.0 — your certainty
If the input is too sparse to classify, return product_type='unknown' and confidence below 0.3. Never invent specs — only label.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You extract product specifications from unstructured text. Return JSON with keys:
  parsed_specs        flat object of normalized specs you found (e.g. {'ram_gb': 16, 'wattage_w': 1200, 'capacity_l': 1.5})
  parsed_source_fields object mapping each spec key to the source field you read it from ('title' | 'description' | 'raw_attributes')
Rules:
  - Use snake_case keys with the unit suffix when meaningful (_gb, _mb, _w, _hz, _l, _kg, _cm, _hours, _years).
  - Numeric values must be numbers, not strings.
  - Only extract specs you can directly justify from the input — never invent.
  - Omit anything you're unsure about; an empty parsed_specs is acceptable.
  - Do NOT include description, title, brand, or category as specs.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: HP Pavilion 170 18.0" 128GB RAM 4096GB SSD
input.description: The HP Pavilion delivers Intel Core Ultra 7 155H performance with Intel Iris Xe graphics. 128GB of RAM and a 4096GB SSD on a 18.0-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 15 hours of battery life. Ships in Midnight.

raw_attributes.description: The HP Pavilion delivers Intel Core Ultra 7 155H performance with Intel Iris Xe graphics. 128GB of RAM and a 4096GB SSD on a 18.0-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 15 hours of battery life. Ships in Midnight.

specialist_capabilities: High multi‑core CPU performance: Intel Core Ultra 7 155H (16 cores, up to 4.8 GHz) for CPU‑bound workloads and heavy multitasking
Very large memory capacity: 128 GB RAM suitable for large in‑memory datasets, many simultaneous VMs/containers, and heavy multitasking
Massive local SSD storage: 4096 GB (4 TB) SSD for large media libraries, local scratch space, and project archives
Integrated GPU (Intel Iris Xe): fine for display, video playback, hardware acceleration and light GPU workloads; not a substitute for a dedicated high‑end GPU for gaming or GPU training
Large high‑resolution display: 18.0" 2560×1600 gives more screen real estate and higher pixel density than 1080p for multitasking and editing
Lightweight for size: listed weight ≈1.21 kg (2.66 lb) — ultraportable class by weight despite the large screen
Long rated battery life: up to 15 hours for extended productivity away from a charger
Windows 11 Pro: business features, domain/enterprise management and security options

specialist_audience.students: Over‑spec for most students but useful for advanced coursework and VM‑heavy development
specialist_audience.professionals: Windows 11 Pro, long battery life and large RAM/storage fit business and productivity needs
specialist_audience.developers: High core count and 128 GB RAM are excellent for local builds, VMs and containerized workflows
specialist_audience.gamers: Integrated Iris Xe allows light gaming, but lacks a discrete GPU for modern AAA titles at high settings
specialist_audience.creators: Large, high‑res screen plus huge RAM and storage suits photo/video editing and large asset workflows; GPU‑accelerated rendering will be limited by lack of discrete GPU
specialist_audience.casual_home: More capacity than typical casual users need; excellent for media and future‑proofing
specialist_audience.power_users: Top fit — very large RAM and storage, high core count, and portable form factor for demanding multitasking

specialist_buyer_questions: Is the 128 GB of RAM user‑upgradable or soldered to the motherboard?
What is the RAM configuration (number of modules, single/dual/quad channel) and memory type/speed?
Is there a discrete GPU option/upgrade or is Intel Iris Xe the only GPU available?
What is the display panel type (IPS, OLED, etc.) and what are measured color coverage and Delta‑E (sRGB/Adobe RGB coverage)?
What ports are available (number/type of USB‑A, USB‑C, Thunderbolt, HDMI, SD card reader, Ethernet)?
Does the USB‑C port support power delivery and/or video output (DisplayPort/Thunderbolt)?
Is the 4096 GB SSD user‑replaceable/upgradeable and is it a single M.2 slot or multiple slots?
What is the sustained CPU performance under load (thermal throttling/noise levels) and fan noise profile?
What is the exact battery capacity (Wh) and does the quoted 'up to 15 hours' reflect a specific test (video/web browsing, etc.)?
What charger wattage is supplied and does it support fast charging?
What wireless connectivity is included (Wi‑Fi 6/6E, Bluetooth version)?
What is the webcam specification (resolution, IR/Windows Hello support) and microphone quality?
Is there an onboard TPM and other business/security features (fingerprint reader, smartcard)?
What are the device dimensions and is the listed weight confirmed with battery and charger?
Is there an OEM warranty and what are the service/repair options and included Windows licensing details?
Is the machine validated for Linux or known compatible distros if I plan to run Linux?

PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

What ports are available (USB-A, USB-C, Thunderbolt, HDMI, Ethernet, SD card) and is there support for charging via USB-C?
How is thermal performance under sustained CPU load (throttling behavior, average surface temps, fan noise)?
Is the device easily serviceable (access to SSD, RAM slots, battery) and what does the warranty cover?
Does the vendor provide drivers or official support for running Linux if needed?
What webcam resolution and microphone quality does the unit have for remote meetings?

input.title: Lenovo IdeaPad 200 16.0" 16GB RAM 1024GB SSD
input.description: The Lenovo IdeaPad delivers AMD Ryzen 5 7640U performance with AMD Radeon 780M graphics. 16GB of RAM and a 1024GB SSD on a 16.0-inch 3840x2400 display. Up to 13 hours of battery life. Ships in Eclipse.

raw_attributes.description: The Lenovo IdeaPad delivers AMD Ryzen 5 7640U performance with AMD Radeon 780M graphics. 16GB of RAM and a 1024GB SSD on a 16.0-inch 3840x2400 display. Up to 13 hours of battery life. Ships in Eclipse.

specialist_capabilities: 6-core AMD Ryzen 5 7640U: solid energy-efficient multi-threaded CPU performance for productivity and multitasking
AMD Radeon 780M integrated graphics: capable of light gaming, esports titles and GPU-accelerated desktop workloads, but not a substitute for a discrete GPU
16 GB RAM: comfortable for multitasking, office suites, web development and light content editing (limits heavy VM/large dataset work)
1 TB SSD: ample fast storage for large file sets, projects and applications
16.0" 3840×2400 display: very high pixel density for detailed workspace and layout — suitable for reading, spreadsheets and detailed image work (panel type/color accuracy not specified)
Up to 13 hours battery life (manufacturer claim): good all-day use potential for travel and meetings
Weight ~3.23 lb (≈1.47 kg): mainstream portable — easier to carry than typical 16" desktop replacements but not ultralight
Windows 11 Pro: business features (BitLocker, domain join) and broad software compatibility
Integrated design tradeoffs: no discrete GPU and RAM is 16 GB (may be soldered) — not optimized for heavy GPU rendering or large-scale ML training
Unspecified I/O and upgradeability in provided data — check ports, expansion slots and RAM storage access before purchase

specialist_audience.students: Large high‑resolution screen and long battery make it good for note-taking, research and media; 16 GB RAM handles multitasking for classes.
specialist_audience.professionals: Windows 11 Pro, 1 TB SSD and long battery life suit office workflows, remote meetings and secure business use.
specialist_audience.developers: 6-core CPU and 16 GB RAM are well‑suited to coding, local builds and light VMs; may be limited for many concurrent heavy containers or large local databases.
specialist_audience.gamers: Suitable for casual and esports titles at modest settings using the integrated 780M; not targeted at high‑end AAA gaming at native resolution.
specialist_audience.creators: High pixel-density 16" screen and GPU acceleration support photo editing and light video work; panel color specs are not provided so not guaranteed for color‑critical professional work.
specialist_audience.casual_home: Good for streaming, web browsing, office apps and media consumption with roomy storage and a sharp display.
specialist_audience.power_users: Performs well for everyday power tasks but lacks a discrete GPU and >16 GB RAM, making it less ideal as a workstation for heavy rendering or ML workloads.

specialist_buyer_questions: What is the display panel type (IPS, OLED, etc.) and measured color coverage (sRGB/AdobeRGB/DCI‑P3)?

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.description: The Lenovo Legion delivers AMD Ryzen 5 7640U performance with Intel Iris Xe graphics. 96GB of RAM and a 512GB SSD on a 16.2-inch 2880x1800 display. Up to 6 hours of battery life. Ships in Black.

raw_attributes.description: The Lenovo Legion delivers AMD Ryzen 5 7640U performance with Intel Iris Xe graphics. 96GB of RAM and a 512GB SSD on a 16.2-inch 2880x1800 display. Up to 6 hours of battery life. Ships in Black.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: Framework Laptop 16 030 14.0" 48GB RAM 1024GB SSD
input.description: The Framework Laptop 16 delivers AMD Ryzen 7 7840HS performance with NVIDIA RTX 4050 graphics. 48GB of RAM and a 1024GB SSD on a 14.0-inch 3024x1964 display. Up to 16 hours of battery life. Ships in Eclipse.

raw_attributes.description: The Framework Laptop 16 delivers AMD Ryzen 7 7840HS performance with NVIDIA RTX 4050 graphics. 48GB of RAM and a 1024GB SSD on a 14.0-inch 3024x1964 display. Up to 16 hours of battery life. Ships in Eclipse.

specialist_capabilities: High-performance CPU + discrete GPU: AMD Ryzen 7 7840HS paired with an NVIDIA RTX 4050 for responsive, GPU-accelerated workloads (gaming, rendering, hardware-accelerated compute).
Large memory and local storage: 48 GB RAM and 1,024 GB SSD for heavy multitasking, large local datasets, and multiple VMs/containers.
High-resolution display: 14.0" 3024×1964 panel (~257 PPI) for dense text, detailed UI work, and high-detail creative review.
Long battery life claim: up to 16 hours—suitable for extended unplugged productivity sessions.
Linux-native: ships with Ubuntu, reducing setup friction for Linux-first developers and sysadmins.
Desktop-replacement portability: at 4.69 lb (≈2.13 kg) this is heavier than ultraportables—trades mobility for performance and battery life.

specialist_audience.students: Powerful hardware can handle demanding coursework and VMs, but weight may be a drawback for daily campus carry.
specialist_audience.professionals: High RAM, fast CPU/GPU and long battery life suit pro workloads and all-day productivity.
specialist_audience.developers: Ships with Ubuntu and has abundant RAM/SSD capacity—good for local development, containers, and CI/test environments.
specialist_audience.gamers: Discrete RTX 4050 enables modern gaming at reasonable settings on a compact screen; expect mid-to-high-quality gameplay.
specialist_audience.creators: High-resolution display plus discrete GPU and large RAM make it fit for photo/video editing and GPU-accelerated creative tools (confirm color gamut).
specialist_audience.casual_home: More performance and memory than most casual users need, but offers long battery life for general use.
specialist_audience.power_users: Spec'd for heavy multitasking, local datasets, and advanced workflows where lots of RAM and a discrete GPU matter.

specialist_buyer_questions: What is the dedicated VRAM (GPU memory) on this NVIDIA RTX 4050 variant?
Is the RAM soldered or user-upgradeable, and how many SO-DIMM slots are present?
Is the SSD removable/upgradeable (M.2 slot available) and what NVMe generation is supported?
What is the display panel type (IPS/OLED) and what color gamut coverage (sRGB / DCI-P3 / AdobeRGB) and factory color accuracy (ΔE) does it have?
What is the display refresh rate (60 Hz, 120 Hz, etc.)?
Which I/O ports are included (Thunderbolt/USB4, USB-C PD, HDMI, full-size USB-A, SD card)?
Does it include a hardware (discrete) MUX switch for direct GPU output vs. integrated graphics switching?
What are the Wi‑Fi and Bluetooth versions supported (Wi‑Fi 6/6E/7, BT 5.2, etc.)?
What is the actual measured battery capacity in Wh and what charging wattage/charger is included?
How well does thermal performance hold under sustained CPU+GPU load (thermal throttling behavior)?
Is there official Linux driver/support documentation from the manufacturer for Ubuntu builds?
What webcam resolution and microphone quality are provided?
Is the keyboard backlit and are there regional layout options?
What warranty, service options, and repairability (e.g., replaceable battery/parts) are offered?
Are there any known issues with running popular creative or ML frameworks on the shipped Ubuntu image?

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: MSI Raider 169 14.2" 24GB RAM 512GB SSD
input.description: The MSI Raider delivers Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite performance with Intel Iris Xe graphics. 24GB of RAM and a 512GB SSD on a 14.2-inch 2880x1800 display. Up to 8 hours of battery life. Ships in Black.

raw_attributes.description: The MSI Raider delivers Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite performance with Intel Iris Xe graphics. 24GB of RAM and a 512GB SSD on a 14.2-inch 2880x1800 display. Up to 8 hours of battery life. Ships in Black.

specialist_capabilities: Productivity & multitasking: 24 GB RAM and a 512 GB SSD for responsive office, browser-heavy, and multitasking workloads.
Portability: 14.2" 2880×1800 panel in a mainstream portable weight class (~4.32 lb / 1.96 kg) with up to ~8 hours battery — suitable for travel and mobile work.
Display: high-resolution 2880×1800 (good pixel density for text, spreadsheets and photo work); useful for detailed viewing though panel type and color gamut are unspecified.
Light creative work: Intel Iris Xe can accelerate photo editing and lighter GPU-accelerated tasks, but this is not a workstation-class GPU for heavy rendering or large video timelines.
Casual gaming only: integrated Intel Iris Xe supports older/indie/tunable titles but no dedicated GPU for modern AAA gaming at high settings.
OS & compatibility note: Ships with Windows 11 Pro on a Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite CPU — check compatibility/emulation for specialized x86 apps or developer toolchains.
Upgrade/IO caution: Storage/RAM upgradability and port layout are not specified — verify upgrade options and available ports for docking and external displays before purchase.

specialist_audience.students: Portable 14.2" form factor, long-ish battery and ample RAM for multitasking and school apps.
specialist_audience.professionals: Windows 11 Pro and 24 GB RAM suit office/productivity workloads and remote work; check app compatibility for any legacy tools.
specialist_audience.developers: Good for web and lightweight development tasks, but verify compatibility of development toolchains with the Qualcomm CPU/Windows build.
specialist_audience.gamers: Not targeted at gamers — integrated Iris Xe only; expect casual/older titles at modest settings.
specialist_audience.creators: Can handle photo editing and light GPU-accelerated tasks; not ideal for heavy 3D rendering or high-resolution video production without a discrete GPU.
specialist_audience.casual_home: Great for browsing, media, and day-to-day use with responsive specs and a high-res screen.
specialist_audience.power_users: 24 GB RAM is attractive, but lack of a discrete GPU and unspecified expandability may limit power-user workflows.

specialist_buyer_questions: Is the Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite implementation ARM-based on this SKU, and are there known compatibility issues with my required x86 applications?
Is the RAM soldered or upgradable/replaceable? If upgradable, what configurations are supported (e.g., single 24 GB module vs. 8+16)?
Is the 512 GB SSD user-replaceable/upgradeable and what interface is it (NVMe PCIe Gen3/Gen4)?
What is the panel type (IPS/OLED) and measured color coverage (sRGB / Adobe RGB / DCI-P3) and peak brightness (nits)?
What is the display refresh rate (60 Hz, 120 Hz, etc.) and is there a touch option?
What ports are exposed (USB-C, Thunderbolt, HDMI, SD card reader, Ethernet via adapter) and what USB-C alternate modes are supported (DisplayPort, power delivery)?
What is the rated charger wattage and how fast does the battery charge? Are the quoted ~8 hours based on real-world mixed usage or a specific test?
What is the exact GPU configuration of the Intel Iris Xe in this unit (execution units / variant)?
What are the thermal characteristics under sustained load — does the CPU/GPU throttle and how noisy are the fans?
Is there TPM/Windows security hardware and what warranty/repair coverage is provided?
Does the laptop include a webcam quality spec (resolution) and microphone performance details?
What Wi‑Fi and Bluetooth versions are supported (Wi‑Fi 6/6E/7, Bluetooth 5.x)?
Is Linux supported or are there known issues running Linux on this model/CPU?
What are the exact physical dimensions and is the weight specified for a typical configuration (battery size included)?
