input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: Framework Laptop 16 091 15.6" 48GB RAM 8192GB SSD
input.description: The Framework Laptop 16 delivers Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite performance with Integrated graphics. 48GB of RAM and a 8192GB SSD on a 15.6-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 8 hours of battery life. Ships in Black.

raw_attributes.description: The Framework Laptop 16 delivers Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite performance with Integrated graphics. 48GB of RAM and a 8192GB SSD on a 15.6-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 8 hours of battery life. Ships in Black.

specialist_capabilities: Handles heavy multitasking and large local datasets — 48 GB RAM and 8,192 GB SSD accommodate many concurrent apps, large VMs, and big media libraries.
Very large onboard storage (8,192 GB) suitable for large project archives, high-capacity photo/video libraries, and local asset workflows.
15.6" 2560×1600 display offers high pixel density (~194 ppi) for detailed work; panel type and color gamut are not specified.
Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite CPU (12 cores, up to 4.0 GHz) provides strong power-efficiency and single-threaded performance on Windows ARM — check app compatibility for x86/x64 titles.
Integrated GPU is adequate for general productivity, web content, and light photo editing/video playback but is not optimized for GPU-accelerated rendering, high-end gaming, or ML model training.
Desktop-replacement portability: ~5.55 lb (≈2.52 kg) and up to 8 hours battery life — solid for workstation-style use but heavier than ultraportables.

specialist_audience.professionals: High RAM, large SSD and a 12‑core CPU suit heavy multitasking, large files, and desktop-replacement workflows.
specialist_audience.developers: Plenty of RAM and CPU cores for local builds, many containers/VMs and multitasking; verify Windows-on-ARM toolchain and dev-tool compatibility.
specialist_audience.creators: Large storage and memory help handle big media projects and local assets, but integrated graphics limit GPU-accelerated rendering workflows.
specialist_audience.power_users: Desktop-class weight and specs support power-user scenarios (many simultaneous apps, big local datasets) where raw GPU power is not required.

specialist_buyer_questions: Is this the ARM-based Snapdragon X Elite model running Windows 11 Home on ARM? Are there known app compatibility issues for the specific software I need (Adobe apps, IDEs, virtualization tools)?
Is the 48 GB of RAM user-upgradeable or soldered? If upgradeable, what is the maximum supported RAM and slot configuration?
Is the 8,192 GB SSD removable/replaceable? What form factor and interface does the internal drive use (e.g., M.2 NVMe)?
What exact display panel type and color coverage does it have (IPS vs OLED, sRGB/Adobe RGB/P3)? What is the measured brightness (nits)?
What ports and expansion options are included (USB-C, USB-A, HDMI, SD card, Thunderbolt/USB4)? Are there modular/upgrade bays or user-replaceable modules?
What is the real-world battery life under different workloads (web browsing, video playback, sustained CPU load)? What charging wattage and fast-charge options are supported?
How does the laptop handle sustained CPU or GPU loads — are there thermal throttling limits and what are typical CPU frequencies under long workloads?
Is the GPU capable of hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding for common codecs (H.264, HEVC, AV1)?
What warranty, repairability, and service options are included? Are parts (battery, screen, keyboard, storage) user-replaceable or available from the vendor?
Does the device support Linux or other OS installs reliably on the Snapdragon X Elite platform (driver availability)?
Is Windows 11 Home a full licensed edition and can it be upgraded to Windows 11 Pro? Are enterprise management features (TPM, BitLocker support) present?
What are the chassis dimensions and exact weight in metric units (kg) for travel and case-fit considerations?

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: HP EliteBook 145 16.0" 24GB RAM 4096GB SSD
input.description: The HP EliteBook delivers AMD Ryzen 9 7940HS performance with Apple Integrated GPU graphics. 24GB of RAM and a 4096GB SSD on a 16.0-inch 3024x1964 display. Up to 20 hours of battery life. Ships in Platinum.

raw_attributes.description: The HP EliteBook delivers AMD Ryzen 9 7940HS performance with Apple Integrated GPU graphics. 24GB of RAM and a 4096GB SSD on a 16.0-inch 3024x1964 display. Up to 20 hours of battery life. Ships in Platinum.

specialist_capabilities: High-end mobile CPU: AMD Ryzen 9 7940HS — strong single- and multi-threaded CPU performance for compute-heavy tasks.
Ultraportable weight class (~2.45 lb / ~1.11 kg) — easy to carry for frequent travelers.
Very long battery life (manufacturer-stated up to 20 hours) — suited to all-day untethered use.
Very large local storage (4096 GB SSD) — keeps large projects, media libraries or datasets on-device.
24 GB RAM — ample for heavy multitasking, large browser/workspace sessions, and development VMs.
High-resolution 16.0" panel (3024 × 1964) — increased workspace and high pixel density useful for detailed work.
Integrated GPU only — adequate for everyday graphics, video playback, and light content editing; limited for GPU-accelerated rendering, gaming, and ML training.

specialist_audience.students: Lightweight and long battery life make it a strong portable choice for classes and study sessions.
specialist_audience.professionals: Business-focused EliteBook design, strong CPU, long battery life and large storage for on-the-go productivity.
specialist_audience.developers: Plenty of RAM and CPU power for local builds, containers and VMs; high-res screen helps with workspace density.
specialist_audience.gamers: Not ideal — integrated GPU limits modern gaming and high-refresh-rate play.
specialist_audience.creators: High-resolution 16" display and large SSD help content workflows, but integrated GPU limits heavy GPU-accelerated rendering and effects.
specialist_audience.casual_home: Excellent for web, streaming and general use thanks to battery life and portability.
specialist_audience.power_users: Strong CPU, 24 GB RAM and 4 TB SSD appeal to power users, but lack of a discrete GPU constrains GPU-heavy workloads.

specialist_buyer_questions: Is the listed GPU actually an integrated graphics solution? Please confirm vendor/model and whether it has dedicated VRAM.
What is the panel type (IPS, OLED, etc.) and is there any factory color calibration or color gamut data (sRGB / AdobeRGB / DCI-P3)?
What is the display refresh rate (60 Hz, 90 Hz, 120 Hz, etc.)?
Are RAM modules soldered or user-upgradeable? If upgradeable, how many slots and max supported RAM?
Is the 4096 GB SSD a single drive or multiple drives, and what interface/generation is it (PCIe Gen3/Gen4/Gen5)?
Are there expansion options (M.2 slots, secondary storage) or an accessible service bay?
What ports are included (Thunderbolt/USB4, USB-A, HDMI, Ethernet, SD card reader)?
Does this model include Windows 11 Pro option or only Windows 11 Home? Is Volume/Enterprise licensing supported?
Does it include business security features (TPM version, fingerprint reader, IR webcam/Windows Hello, smart-card reader)?
What is the rated charging wattage and does it support USB Power Delivery charging?
What is the real-world battery life under common workloads (web browsing, video playback, video conferencing)?
What is the exact weight in kilograms and the chassis dimensions?
What is the sustained CPU performance under load (thermal throttling characteristics and typical sustained clocks)?
How loud are the fans under load (dB levels) and what is thermal performance for prolonged heavy CPU tasks?
Is the keyboard backlit and what is the keyboard layout (US/UK/other)?
Is there an IR or HD webcam and what is its resolution and low-light performance?
Is the SSD encrypted by default or does it support hardware encryption (e.g., Opal)?
Is there a warranty length and what does it cover (on-site, carry-in, accidental damage)?
Does HP supply drivers/firmware for Linux, or is this model known to be Linux-friendly?
Is there any docking compatibility (HP docking stations or USB-C/Thunderbolt docks) and which docks are officially supported?

input.system:
You generate soft 'good_for_*' tags for an e-commerce product. Return JSON with one key:
  good_for_tags    object {tag_key: confidence 0.0-1.0}
Tag key rules:
  - snake_case prefixed with 'good_for_' (e.g. good_for_gaming, good_for_baby_food).
  - Tag vocabulary is open: pick whatever the product genuinely supports.
  - Only emit tags backed by evidence in the input. Lower confidence = weaker evidence; omit tags below 0.2.
  - Cap to ~10 tags. Quality > quantity.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.description: The Lenovo Legion delivers Intel Core i7-13700H performance with AMD Radeon 780M graphics. 96GB of RAM and a 2048GB SSD on a 13.6-inch 3840x2400 display. Up to 7 hours of battery life. Ships in Eclipse.

raw_attributes.description: The Lenovo Legion delivers Intel Core i7-13700H performance with AMD Radeon 780M graphics. 96GB of RAM and a 2048GB SSD on a 13.6-inch 3840x2400 display. Up to 7 hours of battery life. Ships in Eclipse.

specialist_capabilities: High-compute CPU: 14-core Intel Core i7-13700H (up to 5.0 GHz) suited for CPU-heavy multitasking and large spreadsheets
Very large system memory: 96 GB RAM — supports many simultaneous apps, large browser/tab loads, and heavy multitasking without swapping
Large fast storage: 2 TB SSD for large project files, VM images, and local media libraries
High-resolution compact display: 13.6" at 3840×2400 (~333 ppi) provides very fine detail for text and photo work on a small panel
Integrated GPU (AMD Radeon 780M): fine for everyday graphics, video playback, and light/older games at lowered settings — not a high-end discrete gaming/compute GPU
ChromeOS platform: designed for web-first workflows and ChromeOS app ecosystem — may affect availability of some native desktop apps
Portability classification: despite small screen, weight (6.18 lb) places it in a desktop-replacement/heavy mainstream class rather than ultraportable
Battery/runtime expectation: rated up to ~7 hours — moderate battery life; expect shorter runtime under sustained heavy CPU/GPU load
Missing/unspecified items to check before buying: panel type (IPS/OLED), color gamut/accuracy, port selection (Thunderbolt/USB4, HDMI), and upgradeability of RAM/SSD

specialist_audience.students: Very large RAM and storage for heavy multitasking and local projects, but heavy weight may reduce portability for campus carry
specialist_audience.professionals: Strong CPU and large RAM/SSD suit demanding office workflows, large datasets, and multitasking in ChromeOS-friendly environments
specialist_audience.developers: Many cores and abundant RAM support local builds, containers/VMs if using Linux on ChromeOS, but confirm toolchain compatibility
specialist_audience.gamers: Casual or older/less-demanding titles are feasible at reduced settings; not ideal for modern AAA gaming at native resolution
specialist_audience.creators: High pixel density and lots of RAM are good for photo editing and multi-layer projects, but integrated GPU limits heavy GPU-accelerated rendering
specialist_audience.casual_home: Overpowered for basic browsing/media, but offers long-term headroom and large local storage
specialist_audience.power_users: Exceptional RAM and storage capacity appeal to power users, though the integrated GPU and heavy chassis are trade-offs

specialist_buyer_questions: Is the display OLED or IPS, and what is the measured color gamut (sRGB/AdobeRGB/DCI-P3) and color accuracy (ΔE)?
Is the screen touch-capable and does it support HDR?
Are the RAM modules soldered or user-upgradeable? If upgradable, how many slots and maximum supported configuration?
Is the 2 TB SSD removable/upgradeable (M.2 slot)?
What ports are provided (number/type of USB-C, Thunderbolt/USB4 support, HDMI, Ethernet, SD card)?
What is the rated charger wattage and does the laptop support charging over USB-C?
How does the reported ~7 hours battery life vary for web browsing vs video playback vs heavy CPU/GPU workloads?
What is the chassis cooling/noise behavior under sustained CPU load (thermal throttling, fan noise levels)?
Given ChromeOS, which desktop applications do I need that may not be supported natively and do I need Linux/VM support?
Is the webcam quality (resolution, IR for Windows Hello) and does it have a privacy shutter?
Is the keyboard backlit and what is the layout (US/international, presence of NumPad)?
Exact model identifier and warranty length — is this configuration a factory option or a custom reseller build?
Any known driver or compatibility issues with the Intel i7-13700H + AMD Radeon 780M on ChromeOS?

raw_attributes.description: The ASUS VivoBook delivers AMD Ryzen 5 7640U performance with AMD Radeon 780M graphics. 128GB of RAM and a 256GB SSD on a 14.0-inch 2560x1600 display. Up to 13 hours of battery life. Ships in Silver.

specialist_capabilities: Very high multitasking and VM headroom thanks to 128 GB RAM — suitable for heavy multitasking, many browser tabs, and multiple local VMs/containers.
14.0" 2560×1600 (16:10) display — higher pixel density for sharper text and more vertical space than 16:9 at this size.
AMD Ryzen 5 7640U (6 cores, up to 4.9 GHz) — good sustained CPU performance for productivity, compilation, and office workloads on battery and AC.

Integrated AMD Radeon 780M GPU — fine for video playback, GPU-accelerated browser/workstation tasks and light/older games; not a substitute for a discrete GPU for heavy GPU rendering or high‑frame-rate gaming.
Mainstream portability at ~3.65 lb (≈1.65 kg) — suitable for daily commuting without being an ultralight.
Up to 13 hours battery life (manufacturer claim) — supports all-day office/school use in many light-to-moderate workflows.
Ships with Linux (Ubuntu) — good out-of-box fit for developers, sysadmins, and users who prefer Linux environments.
256 GB SSD primary storage — fast boot/response but modest capacity relative to the large RAM; may require external or upgraded storage for large media/libraries.
No displayed specs for ports, panel type (IPS/OLED), or upgradeability in the provided data — verify port selection and whether RAM/storage are user-upgradeable before purchase.

specialist_audience.students: Powerful RAM and long battery life suit demanding multitasking and long study sessions, though 256 GB storage may be limiting for large media projects.
specialist_audience.professionals: Good all-day productivity with strong CPU, abundant RAM for multitasking and VMs, and Linux option for development stacks.
specialist_audience.developers: Ubuntu preinstalled and 128 GB RAM are ideal for running multiple containers/VMs, local databases, and build tooling.
specialist_audience.gamers: Integrated Radeon 780M allows light or older titles at modest settings; not aimed at high-end gaming.
specialist_audience.creators: High-resolution 14" panel and large RAM help photo editing and multitasking; lack of a discrete GPU and unknown color gamut reduce suitability for color-critical or heavy GPU rendering work.
specialist_audience.casual_home: More power and RAM than typical casual users need — excellent responsiveness, but storage size and cost may be overkill.
specialist_audience.power_users: Excellent for users who run many concurrent workloads, VMs, or memory-hungry apps; check upgradeability for long-term storage needs.

You extract product specifications from unstructured text. Return JSON with keys:
  parsed_specs        flat object of normalized specs you found (e.g. {'ram_gb': 16, 'wattage_w': 1200, 'capacity_l': 1.5})
  parsed_source_fields object mapping each spec key to the source field you read it from ('title' | 'description' | 'raw_attributes')
Rules:
  - Use snake_case keys with the unit suffix when meaningful (_gb, _mb, _w, _hz, _l, _kg, _cm, _hours, _years).
  - Numeric values must be numbers, not strings.
  - Only extract specs you can directly justify from the input — never invent.
  - Omit anything you're unsure about; an empty parsed_specs is acceptable.
  - Do NOT include description, title, brand, or category as specs.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

Desktop-replacement weight class (5.91 lbs / ~2.68 kg) — not an ultraportable despite 13.3" screen

specialist_audience.students: Long battery and high RAM make it future-proof for coursework, though the weight may be a downside for daily campus carry.
specialist_audience.professionals: High RAM, large SSD and long battery life suit productivity and remote/field work on Windows applications.
specialist_audience.developers: 32 GB RAM and a high-res display support multitasking, local containers/VMs and dense editor layouts.
specialist_audience.gamers: Can handle casual and esports titles at modest settings via Iris Xe, but lacks a discrete GPU for AAA gaming.
specialist_audience.creators: Good for photo editing and general content work thanks to RAM and resolution, but color gamut/panel specs are unspecified and there's no discrete GPU for heavy rendering.
specialist_audience.casual_home: Smooth for web, media, and everyday apps with excellent battery life.
specialist_audience.power_users: Large RAM and storage satisfy demanding local workloads, but absence of a dedicated GPU and a relatively heavy chassis may limit some high-end workflows.

specialist_buyer_questions: What is the panel type and measured color coverage (sRGB / Adobe RGB / P3) and peak brightness (nits)?

Is the SSD user-replaceable/upgradeable and what interface (PCIe Gen / NVMe) does it use?
Is the 32 GB of RAM soldered or user-upgradeable/replaceable?
What ports are included (Thunderbolt / USB4 / USB-C / HDMI / SD card) and how many?
What is the exact battery capacity (Wh) and is the quoted 22 hours measured under which workload/condition?
What charging wattage/supports fast charging/does it come with the charger?
Does the system throttle under sustained CPU/GPU load — what are typical thermals and fan/noise levels?
Clarify the GPU: how is Intel Iris Xe paired with a Snapdragon X Elite CPU (driver/compatibility implications)?
Is the device fully compatible with Linux distributions and are drivers available for all components?
What is the webcam resolution and presence of microphones / Windows Hello support?
What warranty and repair/support options are included, and is the product modular or designed for easy repairs?

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

You extract product specifications from unstructured text. Return JSON with keys:
  parsed_specs        flat object of normalized specs you found (e.g. {'ram_gb': 16, 'wattage_w': 1200, 'capacity_l': 1.5})
  parsed_source_fields object mapping each spec key to the source field you read it from ('title' | 'description' | 'raw_attributes')
Rules:
  - Use snake_case keys with the unit suffix when meaningful (_gb, _mb, _w, _hz, _l, _kg, _cm, _hours, _years).
  - Numeric values must be numbers, not strings.
  - Only extract specs you can directly justify from the input — never invent.
  - Omit anything you're unsure about; an empty parsed_specs is acceptable.
  - Do NOT include description, title, brand, or category as specs.

input.system:
You extract product specifications from unstructured text. Return JSON with keys:
  parsed_specs        flat object of normalized specs you found (e.g. {'ram_gb': 16, 'wattage_w': 1200, 'capacity_l': 1.5})
  parsed_source_fields object mapping each spec key to the source field you read it from ('title' | 'description' | 'raw_attributes')
Rules:
  - Use snake_case keys with the unit suffix when meaningful (_gb, _mb, _w, _hz, _l, _kg, _cm, _hours, _years).
  - Numeric values must be numbers, not strings.
  - Only extract specs you can directly justify from the input — never invent.
  - Omit anything you're unsure about; an empty parsed_specs is acceptable.
  - Do NOT include description, title, brand, or category as specs.

You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)

Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: Razer Blade 14 160 17.3" 8GB RAM 256GB SSD
input.description: The Razer Blade 14 delivers Intel Core i5-1340P performance with NVIDIA RTX 4050 graphics. 8GB of RAM and a 256GB SSD on a 17.3-inch 3840x2400 display. Up to 15 hours of battery life. Ships in Black.

raw_attributes.description: The Razer Blade 14 delivers Intel Core i5-1340P performance with NVIDIA RTX 4050 graphics. 8GB of RAM and a 256GB SSD on a 17.3-inch 3840x2400 display. Up to 15 hours of battery life. Ships in Black.

specialist_capabilities: Mid-range gaming capable: includes an NVIDIA RTX 4050 GPU suitable for 1080p or moderate-quality 1440p gaming, but overall gaming headroom is limited by 8 GB system RAM.
High-resolution 17.3" 3840×2400 panel provides very high pixel density for detailed content — color gamut and panel type not specified and should be verified for professional color work.
Intel Core i5-1340P (12 cores) offers efficient multi-threaded performance for productivity and light content-creation tasks.
Long vendor-claimed battery life (up to 15 hours) for light use; real-world battery under load will be lower.
Desktop-replacement weight class (~4.5 lb / ~2.0 kg): large-screen workload focus rather than ultraportable travel use.
256 GB SSD provides fast storage but is small for games, large photo/video projects, or multiple VM images — expect to upgrade or add storage.
Ships with Windows 11 Pro — includes business features and enterprise management compatibility.
8 GB RAM is a bottleneck for heavy multitasking, serious content creation, or modern gaming; add RAM for a noticeably better experience.

specialist_audience.students: Large screen and long battery help study and media; 8 GB RAM and 256 GB storage may be limiting — better for students who prioritize screen size over portability.
specialist_audience.professionals: Windows 11 Pro, efficient CPU and dedicated GPU are useful for business apps and light GPU-accelerated tasks; storage/RAM may need upgrading.
specialist_audience.developers: Capable CPU and GPU for local builds and testing; 8 GB RAM constrains running many containers/VMs — recommend adding RAM for heavier dev work.
specialist_audience.gamers: RTX 4050 and 17.3" display suit casual to mid-range gamers, but limited RAM and unknown display refresh rate reduce competitiveness for high-FPS play.
specialist_audience.creators: High-resolution screen and discrete GPU help photo/video editing and GPU rendering; uncertain panel color accuracy and low system RAM make it suboptimal for pro workflows without upgrades.
specialist_audience.casual_home: Great for streaming, web, and media consumption due to large high-res screen and long battery life for light use.
specialist_audience.power_users: Likely not ideal out of the box because 8 GB RAM and 256 GB storage will constrain power users who run many concurrent heavy applications.

specialist_buyer_questions: How much VRAM does the RTX 4050 have in this specific configuration?
Is the display OLED or IPS, and what coverage does it have for sRGB / DCI-P3 / Adobe RGB?
What is the panel's refresh rate (60 Hz, 120 Hz, etc.)?
Is the RAM soldered or are there user-accessible SODIMM slots for upgrading to 16/32 GB?
Is the 256 GB SSD NVMe (PCIe) and is it replaceable/expandable?
What ports are included? (Thunderbolt 4 / USB-C PD, HDMI version, Ethernet, SD card slot)
What is the AC adapter wattage and expected charging time?
What is the webcam resolution and microphone quality?
How does the laptop perform thermally under sustained CPU/GPU load (throttling behavior)?
Is the stated 15-hour battery life a vendor claim for light use, and what is typical battery on mixed or gaming workloads?
What are the exact chassis dimensions and the confirmed weight in kg/lbs?
Is the keyboard backlit (per-key RGB or single-zone) and what is the layout?
Is there an active warranty and what does it cover (manufacturer vs. retailer)?
Are drivers and support available for running Linux if needed?
Are speakers and trackpad quality suitable for media/remote meetings?

input.system:
You generate soft 'good_for_*' tags for an e-commerce product. Return JSON with one key:
  good_for_tags    object {tag_key: confidence 0.0-1.0}
Tag key rules:
  - snake_case prefixed with 'good_for_' (e.g. good_for_gaming, good_for_baby_food).
  - Tag vocabulary is open: pick whatever the product genuinely supports.
  - Only emit tags backed by evidence in the input. Lower confidence = weaker evidence; omit tags below 0.2.
  - Cap to ~10 tags. Quality > quantity.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.

creators, casual_home, power_users.

input.title: Apple MacBook Pro 096 15.6" 24GB RAM 1024GB SSD

24 GB unified RAM and 1 TB SSD for responsive multitasking and storing large project files.
15.6" 2880×1800 high-resolution display suited to detailed photo/video work and dense UI layouts.
Integrated Apple GPU provides good GPU-accelerated performance for editing, encoding, and many creative effects (not a discrete GPU).
Mainstream-portable weight (~1.8 kg / 3.96 lb) with up to 16 hours battery life — suitable for travel and all-day mobile use.
macOS Sonoma environment with native support for Apple ecosystem apps and developer tools.
Not optimal for workloads that specifically require a discrete GPU or >=32 GB RAM (e.g., large-scale ML training or worst-case GPU rendering).

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

specialist_capabilities: High-performance mobile CPU: Intel Core i7-13700H (14 cores, up to 5.0 GHz) for multithreaded productivity and compilation tasks
Long claimed battery life: up to 17 hours for extended unplugged use (test conditions unspecified)
Very large onboard storage (listed as 8192 GB SSD) for large local datasets, media libraries, or many VMs
13.6" 1920×1200 display: higher-than-1080p vertical resolution that benefits document editing and productivity
Mainstream portability: ~4.37 lbs (~1.98 kg) — lighter than typical desktop-replacement but not an ultraportable
Windows 11 Pro out of the box: suited for corporate environments and business security/policy features
Integrated GPU only (listed as Apple Integrated GPU): suitable for general UI acceleration and lightweight graphics tasks, not for GPU-accelerated rendering or heavy gaming

specialist_audience.students: Long battery life and portable 13.6" size with Windows Pro for school apps and productivity.
specialist_audience.professionals: Powerful 14-core CPU and Windows 11 Pro make it suitable for office and corporate workloads.
specialist_audience.developers: Strong multicore CPU for local builds; 16GB RAM is adequate for many dev workflows but may limit large container/VM setups.
specialist_audience.creators: Capable for light photo/video editing, but integrated GPU and 16GB RAM limit heavy GPU rendering or large video projects.
specialist_audience.casual_home: Large storage and long battery life suit media, browsing, and everyday tasks.
specialist_audience.power_users: Good CPU and huge listed storage, but integrated GPU and 16GB RAM may restrict advanced GPU-accelerated or ML workflows.

specialist_buyer_questions: The GPU is listed as "Apple Integrated GPU" — is this correct? If so, what is the actual GPU model and expected graphics performance?
Storage is listed as 8192 GB (8 TB). Is that the factory-configured SSD capacity or a typo? Is the SSD user-replaceable/expandable?
Is the 16GB of RAM soldered or are there additional SO-DIMM slots for upgrading to 32GB+?
What is the display panel type (IPS/OLED/TN) and what color coverage (sRGB/AdobeRGB) does it provide?
What ports are included (USB-A, USB-C, Thunderbolt 4, HDMI, SD card) and what are the charging/power delivery specs?
What is the tested battery life under real-world conditions (web browsing, video playback, mixed use) and what battery capacity (Wh) is installed?
What is the exact weight and chassis dimensions? Are there lighter configurations if travel weight is a priority?
Is the SSD NVMe (PCIe) and how many M.2 slots are available for storage expansion?
What thermal solution / cooling does the chassis use, and how does performance hold under sustained multithreaded load?
Is there an official service manual or information on how serviceable/upgradable the unit is (battery, SSD, RAM, Wi‑Fi module)?
What webcam resolution and microphone quality are included (important for remote meetings)?
Is Windows 11 Pro activated and does the unit include any OEM software or enterprise management features (TPM, vPro)?
What warranty and business support options are available (on-site, next-business-day, extended plans)?
Are there docking or enterprise connectivity options supported (Dell dock compatibility, WWAN support)?
What accessories are included (charger wattage, power adapter size)?
Confirm release year/model variant and whether newer Precision/13.6" models with discrete GPUs or higher RAM configs exist.

input.system:
You are the composer agent: the single writer of one product's canonical catalog row. You read the raw product, the findings emitted by every upstream agent that actually ran, and decide which keys belong on the canonical row.

Policy:
  1. No hallucination. Only include a key if its value is grounded in the raw row, the parsed specs, or the scraped findings. If a buyer question (specialist_buyer_questions) has no grounded answer in those sources, DO NOT fabricate one — omit the key.
  2. Overlap resolution. Apply the per-key precedence below. Record the chosen source in composer_decisions; put the dropped value in dropped_alternatives.
  3. Echo discipline. A grounded value STILL belongs on the canonical row even if raw_attributes already has it — downstream readers must not have to re-join raw to reconstruct canonical state. Echo only matters for measuring agent value-add, and goes in composer_decisions[*].reason='echoes_raw', not into dropped keys.
  4. Type discipline. Scalars stay scalars; never wrap a number in an object. For spec dicts (parsed_specs, scraped_specs), flatten their inner keys up into composed_fields one level.
  5. Narrative text (prose bullets, audience blurbs, buyer questions) does NOT belong on the canonical row — the Python post-check will drop any narrative key regardless.
  6. Only reference strategies listed in the 'available_findings' section of the user prompt. If a strategy didn't run, do not invent a source_strategy for it.
  7. 1:1 requirement. For every key you emit in `composed_fields`, you MUST emit a matching entry in `composer_decisions` with `key` equal to that field name. Do not ship a composed value without a decision — the Python post-check will synthesize missing decisions and flag the gap, but a missing decision breaks cell lineage in the inspector.

Per-key precedence:
  - product_type / taxonomy_path -> taxonomy_v1 (canonical classifier)
  - numeric/categorical specs (ram_gb, weight_kg, etc.) -> scraper_v1 beats parser_v1 when both are present (scraper reads the manufacturer page, parser is LLM extraction); parser_v1 beats all others
  - good_for_* tags -> soft_tagger_v1
  - specialist_use_case_fit (structured {use_case: confidence}) may be included; specialist narrative is never canonical
If two sources conflict, pick one and record the dropped alternative in composer_decisions[*].dropped_alternatives.

Return JSON with two keys:
  composed_fields     object {key: value} — the canonical row content
  composer_decisions  list of objects {key, chosen_value, source_strategy, reason, dropped_alternatives}. Every key in composed_fields MUST have a matching decision whose chosen_value equals the composed_fields value; the Python post-check will drop any decision that lies about its own key.

Rules:
  - Use snake_case keys with the unit suffix when meaningful (_gb, _mb, _w, _hz, _l, _kg, _cm, _hours, _years).
  - Numeric values must be numbers, not strings.
  - Only extract specs you can directly justify from the input — never invent.
  - Omit anything you're unsure about; an empty parsed_specs is acceptable.
  - Do NOT include description, title, brand, or category as specs.

input.system:
You are a domain specialist enriching a product entry for an e-commerce catalog. Return JSON with keys:
  specialist_capabilities    list[str] — concise capability statements tailored to the product type (no marketing fluff)
  specialist_use_case_fit    object {use_case: confidence 0.0-1.0} — only the use cases this specific product genuinely fits
  specialist_audience        object {audience: short_reason}
  specialist_buyer_questions list[str] — questions a real buyer of this product type would ask before purchase
Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.

Never invent specs the input does not support. Use the product-type guidance below to weight what matters most.
PRODUCT TYPE GUIDANCE:
Laptops. Capability bullets that matter most:
- Workload fit: gaming (GPU class + thermal headroom), ML/training (VRAM,
  dedicated GPU, RAM ≥ 32GB), creative (color-accurate display, GPU rendering),
  web/office (battery life + portability), school/general (cost + battery).
- Portability: weight class (ultraportable < 1.4kg, mainstream 1.4–2.0kg,
  desktop replacement > 2.0kg). Battery hours matter more than mAh.
- Display: panel type (OLED/IPS/TN), refresh rate (120Hz+ for gaming),
  resolution per inch.
- I/O: Thunderbolt presence, dock-friendliness, removable storage / RAM
  upgradeability.
- OS lock-in: macOS / Windows / ChromeOS / Linux compatibility.

Use cases worth scoring (omit ones the product doesn't fit): gaming,
machine_learning, creative, web_dev, school, business, travel, general.
Audiences worth labeling: students, professionals, developers, gamers,
creators, casual_home, power_users.
